macro_segments.title: Contradiction between Mishnayot on Borrowing and the Status of the Borrower Upon Return

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the problem: Mishnah on craftsmen as paid bailees contradicts Mishnah on borrowing
Citation of the relevant Mishnah from Bava Metzia 98b on borrowing and returning through agents
Rashi's explanation of the distinction between borrowing and returning with agreed-upon agents
Resolution via Efrayim bar Pappa: The contradiction applies only during the borrowing period
Challenge from Rav Nachman bar Pappa: Cases where the borrower declares work is finished
Distinction between 'Take your thing' with payment demand and simple completion statement
Gemara's logical inference: Why state the explicit formulation if simple completion suffices?
Alternative explanation: Tosefta versions show the specific formulation is primary teaching point
Manuscript evidence and reconstruction: Possible textual transmission errors between sources
Final resolution: The explicit formula teaches what simple completion alone does not

macro_segments.title: Hona Mar bar Amemar's Question on the Borrower's Liability for Theft and Loss Upon Return

micro_segments.title: New question: When a borrower becomes exempt from accidents, does he lose all paid-bailee status?
Amemar's position: The borrower retains paid-bailee status due to ongoing benefit from use
Baraita of the artisan and father-in-law: Distinction between outgoing and return journey
Analysis of benefit (hana'ah) in return phase and paid-bailee classification

macro_segments.title: Case of the Donkey Sale and the Injured Animal on Return

micro_segments.title: Setup: Bailee tries to sell donkey, fails, and animal is injured on return journey
Rav Nachman's ruling: The bailee is liable because return is same as outgoing mission
Rava's challenge: The baraita teaches exemption on return, not liability
Rav Nachman's clarification: This return is continuous with the sale mission, so still outgoing status
Modern analogy: Rental car liability extensions and lease-period boundaries
Summary of agreement-based exemption and period-boundary principles

macro_segments.title: Guard-for-Guard Reciprocal Arrangement and Its Classification

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: 'Guard for me and I'll guard for you' creates paid-bailee status
Problem: How can this be paid bailment if owner is simultaneously working for bailee?
Rav Papa's resolution: The arrangement is sequential—today I guard, tomorrow you guard
Baraita parallels: Lending and guarding can be mixed with same effect if sequential
Crucial principle: Paid-bailee status requires non-overlapping employment times

macro_segments.title: Case of the Perfume Makers' Coat: Liability During Explicit Employment Arrangement

micro_segments.title: First scenario: One baker asked to bake, coat stolen before return, Rav Papa rules liable
Rabbis' challenge: If guarding occurred during his employment (mishmeeret ba'alim), should be exempt
Resolution: Investigation reveals he was drinking, not yet in their employment when theft occurred
Second scenario: Baker explicitly conditions wage on reciprocal guarding of coat
Same resolution: Theft occurred during a break, so he had no employment status at that moment
Interpretation: Validates the principle that mishmeeret ba'alim requires simultaneous actual employment
Extended examples: Contractor guarding belongings only during actual work hours, not breaks

macro_segments.title: Case of the Tall and Short Travelers: Unilateral Assumption of Guardianship

micro_segments.title: Setup: Tall traveler on donkey, short traveler walking; they exchange items at river
Action: Short traveler places coat on donkey, takes tall traveler's linen sheet, sheet washes away
Rabba's ruling: Tall traveler liable because short traveler was guarding his coat in exchange
Rabbis' objection: This is a sheilah ba'ba'alim (guarding while being guarded for), so should be exempt
Resolution: Short traveler placed coat on donkey without tall traveler's knowledge or consent
Learned principle: A shomer she-lo mi-da'at (unwitting bailee) has no protected status

macro_segments.title: Case of the Donkey Hire and the Wrong Road: Breach of Instructions and Causation

micro_segments.title: Setup: Owner instructs renter to avoid water-filled road; renter ignores and takes wrong road
Renter's claim: Although I took wrong road, there was no water there, so not my fault
Rabba's initial analysis: The sworn statement deserves credibility via miggo principle
Abaye's refutation: Miggo does not apply when public knowledge contradicts the claim
Principle: Anan sahadei (public knowledge) overrides miggo logic in bailment disputes

macro_segments.title: Statements of Acceptance and Rejection of Guardianship Responsibility

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: 'Guard for me' with response 'Put it in front of me' = unpaid bailee
Rav Huna's interpretation: 'Put in front of you' is rejection, creating no bailee status
Unresolved question: What is the status when someone simply says 'Put' without qualification?
Textual reconciliation: Mishnah contrasts 'Put in front of me' (reject) with 'Put' (accept)

macro_segments.title: Bringing Objects into Courtyard: Implicit vs. Explicit Acceptance of Guardianship

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic dispute: Whether bringing items into guarded courtyard requires explicit acceptance
Rabbanan's position: Bringing into courtyard with permission creates unpaid-bailee status
Rabbi's position: Explicit acceptance of guardianship must be stated to incur liability
First explanation: Courtyard is inherently guarded, so permission to enter implies acceptance
Second explanation: Permission needed to enter courtyard, so if granted, acceptance is implied
Marketplace contrast: In public places, permission to put down items implies acceptance (against Rabbi)
Conclusion: Different rules apply based on whether location is inherently guarded or requires consent

topical_tags.term: sequence of obligations (stam gamarti, tol et shelcha)
agreement on method of return (bekach amar lo)
akin to continuous mission (azil v'la izbaban)
